title: Size characterisation of TiO2 nanoparticles present in the sugar coating of chewing gum (and similar confectionery products) by centrifugal liquid sedimentation
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"apxjl","text":"Titanium dioxide (TiO2, INS No 171) is a still widely applied food colorant outside the European Union. The possible health risk related to the consumption of TiO2 is most probably also related to its particle size distribution. Centrifugal liquid sedimentation (CLS) is a fast and robust method that can be applied for high-resolution particle size distribution measurement of various nanomaterials. It was successfully applied in the characterisation of food-grade titanium dioxide extracted from sugar-coated confectionery products, such as chewing gum. This protocol summarises the details of such a disk centrifuge based analysis procedure and discusses also the limitations of the method.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sub","offset":21,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":162,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"oikn2","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"mx9fr","text":"Titanium dioxide is banned as food additive in the European Union since 20221. Since February 2023, a similar ban exists in the states that are members of to the Gulf Standards Organisation but it is still considered to be safe in many other countries, including the US, Canada, China, Japan and the UK.2,3 Debate on the safety of E171 in food products is still ongoing mainly because of the size of TiO2 particles that fall in the nanometric size range in this colorant.4,5,6 Apart of the chemical composition, just like in case of other materials, the size of the titanium oxide particles might be a key factor regarding toxicity.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":76,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":303,"length":3},{"style":"sup","offset":471,"length":5},{"style":"sub","offset":403,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bivcr","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bdtla","text":"There are numerous techniques available for measuring the size distribution of (nano)particles, ranging from user friendly and relatively economic, batch measurement methods like dynamic light scattering, to high-end, expensive but low throughput methods like transmission electron microscopy. Centrifugal liquid sedimentation (CLS) analysis by using a disk centrifuge is a middle ground solution. It provides excellent resolution combined with good throughput and a moderate investment as instrument price. It allows studying particles in suspension, what fits the procedure of extracting the particles from the food product. On the other hand, similarly to other detection techniques relying on light extinction it needs optical input parameters in order to generate mass or number based size distributions from extinction based size distribution. Additionally it needs particle density as input parameter, because it operates on the principle of separating particles by size using centrifugal sedimentation in a liquid medium. Sedimentation time corresponds to the settling velocity, which depends on particle size, shape, and density.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"xa6qf","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"tiote","text":"In a typical CLS experiment using a disk centrifuge (Figure 2), the centrifugal sedimentation is carried out in a density gradient, to avoid the sedimentation of the particle suspension through the liquid layer without particle size separation. The density of the liquid is increasing from the top layer to the direction of the perimeter of the disc. The density gradient can be created from various density sucrose solutions in water (in non-aqueous systems various mixtures of liquids can be used). When a small volume of the sample is injected, the suspension might have higher average density than the pure solvent but the fluid under the surface has also higher density. This way there is no driving force for bulk settling of the suspension, particles follow the Stokes law while settling through the fluid of increasing density.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8h3hs","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"p5tfb","text":"Stokes law is applied to determine the size distribution of spherical particles by measuring the time required for the particles to settle a known distance under known gravitational force in a liquid of known density and viscosity.  In a disc centrifuge, centrifugal acceleration replaces the gravitational force and diameter (D) of the particles can be calculated from their sedimentation time (t) as follows:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"yrqe4","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"0togv","text":"D = [(18ηln(Rf/R0)/((ρp-ρf)ω2t)]0.5","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":14},{"style":"italic","offset":13,"length":1},{"style":"italic","offset":14,"length":2},{"style":"italic","offset":16,"length":1},{"style":"italic","offset":17,"length":6},{"style":"italic","offset":22,"length":1},{"style":"italic","offset":23,"length":3},{"style":"italic","offset":25,"length":1},{"style":"italic","offset":26,"length":3},{"style":"italic","offset":28,"length":1},{"style":"italic","offset":29,"length":3},{"style":"italic","offset":32,"length":3},{"style":"sup","offset":28,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":32,"length":3},{"style":"sub","offset":13,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":16,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":22,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":25,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ffduk","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dmdh8","text":"where","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"onxii","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"eey9g","text":"D is the particle diameter","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"0fgln","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"wkkt9","text":"η is the fluid viscosity","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"a8nym","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"zxtqj","text":"Rf is the final radius of rotation (distance of the detector from center of is the initial radius of rotation (distance of the fluid surface from the center of rotation)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sub","offset":1,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"vx8ck","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"0jxod","text":"ρp is the rotational velocity","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bbwwo","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ejh6a","text":"t is the time required to sediment from R0 to Rf.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sub","offset":41,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":47,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"xcgiw","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"gaokf","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7jda8","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dvonn","text":"If a centrifuge is running at constant speed and temperature, all of the parameters are constant during a typical analysis. They can be combined in one single constant that can be determined by measuring the sedimentation time of a calibration standard with known size (narrow size distribution) and known density. Thus, the equation above is simplified to","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"plf4a","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dhh0x","text":"D = K(1/t)0.5","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":10},{"style":"italic","offset":10,"length":3},{"style":"sup","offset":10,"length":3}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"oy3lq","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"f8cwv","text":"where K is a combination of constants and t is the time the particles need to reach the detector.7","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":97,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"pprc2","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1il05","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dhmle","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"kk28p","text":"Disc centrifuges consist of a hollow, transparent disc that is usually loaded with the density gradient fluid. During rotation, the injected liquid gradient steps form a fluid layer on which a thin layer of the particle suspension is injected at the centre of the disc. The particles migrate according to their settling velocity to the perimeter of the disc. All particles of a certain size fraction (or settling velocity) move in a narrow band with growing distance from the initial position. In the case of polydisperse particle systems, several of such particle fraction bands are moving to the direction of the detector, similarly to chromatographic separation techniques. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"wdlxs","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1lbq8","text":"The particles of various size arrive to the light beam at various time points. Particle diameters are calculated from sedimentation times considering the sedimentation time of the calibration standard. This allows taking into account any variation of the fluid gradient during subsequent experiments.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"84pqh","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"tloly","text":"The detector reads initial light intensity and this signal is reduced when the particle fractions pass in front of the light beam. The reduction of intensity due to absorption and scattering indicates the concentration of particles. When a monochromatic light source (laser) is used, concentration of particles of a specific size can be calculated applying the Mie theory. When all particles pass the detector, the signal returns to original level. Plotting the particle concentration against the particle diameter produces a differential distribution. Cumulative distribution is then generated by integration of the differential distribution curve (Figures 4-5).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"0d35m","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Switch on the instrument and verify that the disc is clean. The instrument (laser light source) needs to warm up to provide stable results. The sample preparation time together with the 40 minutes used for gradient stabilisation (see later) are sufficient to warm up the instrument. However, if the instrument is used on consecutive days, it is beneficial to keep the instrument switched on.
Using an analytical balance, measure 2 g of sucrose in a centrifuge tube and add 23 mL of ultrapure water using a bottle top dispenser to prepare the 8% sucrose solution.
Using an analytical balance, measure 6 g of sucrose in a centrifuge tube and add 19 mL of ultrapure water to prepare the 24% sucrose solution. Wait until the sucrose is dissolved in both tubes.
Prepare nine, 1.6 mL final volume gradient steps as shown in Figure 1 by mixing the two different sucrose solutions in 2 mL Eppendorf tubes as shown in Figure 1.

Close the disk of the instrument with the disc closure. Check position of the mark for best balancing. Inject the first step of the gradient with a 5 mL syringe while the disc is not rotating. Close the door of the instrument, set manually 22000 rotational velocity and start rotation. When the final velocity is reached, inject the remaining 8 steps of the gradient (in the order of decreasing density). Always clean the needle of the syringe from drops with a paper tissue before injection. Inject 0.5 mL dodecane on top to avoid change of the fluid gradient due to evaporation of water during the experiment. Wait 40 minutes for gradient stabilisation.

Place 3 chewing gums in a 50 mL centrifuge tube and add 25 mL of ultrapure water. Dissolve the white coating layer by gentle moving of the tube. The extraction can be considered complete when only a dark-grey/brownish core remains.
Remove the gum from the extract suspension. This is done by pouring the whole extract through a funnel (without filter) into a new 50 mL tube.
Rinse the retained materials with ultrapure water and bring the obtained suspension to a final volume of 35 mL.
Vortex-stir the tube for 30 seconds and transfer a 10 mL aliquot into 15 mL Falcon tube. Bath-sonicate for 10 minutes and dilute 1:30 with ultrapure water before CLS analysis. 8

Set the measurement method as illustrated in Figure 3. These input data foresee a measurement in 20 – 5000 nm size range. Density and optical parameters suppose anatase crystal form for TiO 2 . (In case you have Raman spectroscopy or other data that suggest that the crystal form is rutile, the corresponding density and optical parameter values should be applied).

Follow the instructions of the instrument appearing on the screen to perform measurements. Pour 2-5 mL of the calibration standard suspension in a 15 mL centrifuge tube. Use 100 µL of 236 nm PVC standard without any dilution for calibration before each measurement. For best reading of the volume and injection efficiency, leave an air bubble above the liquid in the syringe and inject it with a fast movement starting the data collection at the same time. Wait until calibration run is finished and the baseline returns to the original level. Take 100 µL of suspension containing the nanoparticles extracted from the sample using a 1 mL syringe; leave an air bubble above the liquid in the syringe. Clean the syringe needle from apparent drops using paper tissue wiper. Inject the sample with a fast movement and start data collection at the same time.
Perform at least three repetitions.

Use the “Retrieve distribution” menu point of the main menu to select the results from the actual method folder. Results can be plotted in various ways (absorption, weight, surface or number distribution, differential or cumulative distribution, linear or logarithmic x-scale, etc.) by selecting functions in the data visualisation window. Multiple separate measurements can be selected and compared in the same window.

Results can be exported as screenshots or saved as coma separated value (CSV) files. The saved CSV files can be opened in Excel and data can be plotted using the diameter values as independent variable (x values) and the selected differential distributions as y values. Care should be taken at data visualisation by setting linear or logarithmic scale depending on the plotted column and applying the proper dimension label for the y-axis.
Clean the disk by sucking out the collapsed gradient liquid from the disk without removing the disc closure.
Then remove the closure.

Fill it up to the third with MilliQ water using a wash bottle. Rotate the disc manually. Suck the liquid from the disk. Fill it up to the third with ethanol using a wash bottle. Rotate the disc manually. Suck the liquid from the disk. Repeat it three times.
Absorb the remaining liquid from the disc with soft tissue paper.
Clean the surface of the disc with soft tissue paper, first wet with water, then with ethanol.

Problem 1 Calibration fails. Solution Double check calibration standard size and the parameters introduced to create the method. Use a fresh aliquot from the calibration standard suspension. Shake or shortly sonicate it before use. Stop the centrifuge, clean the disc and start again by re-building the sucrose gradient. Problem 2 The peak of the calibration standard appears at a wrong size. Solution It is normal that at the first run the size appears at a value that does not perfectly match the expected one. At the second run – using the same procedure – the peak should appear very close to the expected size. Problem 3 Particles in the larger than 1 µm size range are just partially detected (as shown in Figure 4), information would be needed also about larger particles. Solution Repeat the experiment at slower rotation speed. The procedure definition page of the software helps you with recommended speed and run time estimation. Decreased rotation speed will increase the measurement time. Unfortunately, you will also need to rebuild the gradient. Problem 4 There is a need to measure also the contribution of particles below 20 nm. Solution Change the minimum diameter setting of the procedure accordingly. It will increase the measurement time. Problem 5 The exported curves are different from the curves shown by the instrument on the screen Solution Double-check if you are comparing absorbance, mass or number distributions and if you have used the right - linear or logarithmic - scale. If all fits, the reason might be related to the next points. Problem 6 The exported absorbance based distribution values show a strong drift. Solution The exported absorbance based distribution values are not baseline subtracted. You can generate baseline values for the absorbance-based distribution by plotting on linear scale the absorbance values against the time points and fitting a linear on the first and last data point. You can calculate baseline values for all time points using the resulting equation. Problem 7 The plotted cumulative curves are not monotonic. Solution Do not use the exported cumulative results as they are most probably generated without excluding possible negative values in the differential distribution. As a result, the plotted curves that are based on exported data are often not monotonic. You can generate your own cumulative curves by approximating the integrand using the trapezoidal rule and excluding negative results and adding instead 0 when generating cumulative results. In excel it can be done for example by using the IF(logical test, [value if true], [value if false] logical function. Problem 8 The disc has radial, scratch or fracture looking lines on the surface Solution The lines are most probably made of dried sugar. Clean it with a soft paper tissue soaked in water, and then wipe it with a second tissue wet of ethanol. At next experiment make sure to dry the needle before introducing it in the centrifuge when building the sucrose gradient.
Total time of the experiment is expected to be 2-3h. Additional measurements take less time. Total life time of a gradient is about 4-8 hours.
Typical results are shown in the figures 4 and 5.

Figure 1. Preparation of gradient steps Figure 2. Schematic front view of the disc of a CLS instrument Figure 3. Screenshot showing the details of the measurement method Figure 4. Light extinction based differential and cumulative size distribution of TiO 2 nanoparticles extracted from chewing gum Figure 5. Mass (left) and number (right) weighted differential and cumulative size distributions of TiO 2 nanoparticles extracted from chewing gum
